item.text: I’ll use the phase progress tracker because this is phase-plan work, then I’ll read the briefed contract files and only edit the schema, fixtures, and tests needed for this task.

item.aggregated_output:
---
name: progress-md-phase-tracker
description: Create and maintain `PROGRESS.md` for phase-based implementation work. Use when executing a phase plan, before starting phase implementation, during long or multi-step work, after validation, when a phase is complete, or when future/out-of-scope tasks should be recorded for later phases.
---

# PROGRESS.md Phase Tracker

## Overview

Treat `PROGRESS.md` as a living phase checkpoint artifact. Create or update it before execution starts, refresh it during meaningful checkpoints, and finalize it only after checking the completed work against the phase plan.

## Before Execution

1. Read the active phase plan in `phase-plans/` and any referenced standards or concept docs.
2. Inspect existing `PROGRESS.md` if present. Preserve prior phase history and user-written notes.
3. Add or update an entry for the active phase before editing implementation files.
4. Record the planned execution shape, not just the final goal.

Use this minimum structure when creating a new file:

```markdown
# Progress

## <PHASE-ID> - <phase title>

Status: Planned | In progress | Validation blocked | Complete | Partial
Started: <YYYY-MM-DD>
Last updated: <YYYY-MM-DD>

Plan:
- <planned step or deliverable>

Current checkpoint:
- <what has been done so far or what is about to start>

Validation:
- <planned command/check> -> not run yet

Out-of-scope / future work:
- <future task, reason it is out of current phase, suggested phase if known>
```

Use the repository's existing `PROGRESS.md` style if one already exists.

## During Execution

Update `PROGRESS.md` at meaningful checkpoints:

- After planning is translated into concrete steps.
- After each major deliverable or subtask completes.
- Before switching from implementation to validation.
- After validation passes, fails, or is blocked.
- When discovering a scope mismatch, prerequisite gap, or environment blocker.
- Before final response if the user asked for frequent progress updates.

Keep entries factual. Do not mark a step complete until the relevant files, artifacts, or checks support it.

## Completion Update

When the whole phase appears complete:

1. Re-read the phase plan and compare it to the implementation.
2. Update `PROGRESS.md` with final status, completed deliverables, validation results, and any deviations.
3. If validation is blocked, mark the phase `Validation blocked` or `Partial`, not `Complete`.
4. Include exact commands run and short results.
5. Include generated evidence paths when the phase produces traces, scorecards, reviews, changelogs, or similar artifacts.

Do not claim phase completion in chat until `PROGRESS.md` reflects the same state.

## Out-of-Scope And Future Work

When work is useful but outside the current phase:

- Do not implement it unless the user expands scope.
- Add it under `Out-of-scope / future work`.
- State why it is outside the current phase.
- Link it to a future phase or category when obvious.
- Keep it separate from blockers. A future task should not make the current phase incomplete unless the phase plan requires it.

Example:

```markdown
Out-of-scope / future work:
- Add browser UI polish. Outside PHASE-04A because this phase only defines ASCII rendering; candidate for PHASE-08A.
- Add extra enemy archetypes. Outside current minimal dungeon scope; candidate for enemy variety phase.
```

## Edit Rules

- Preserve existing progress history.
- Prefer appending or updating the active phase section over rewriting the whole file.
- Keep timestamps concrete using the current local date.
- Separate planned, in-progress, complete, blocked, and future-work states.
- Avoid optimistic language that is not backed by files, tests, or artifacts.

# ENVIRONMENT.md — Living Environment Facts (STEP 0)

The single source of environment truth, pasted (or linked as mandatory reading) at
the top of **every** task brief as its STEP 0 block. Every fact a worker rediscovers
mid-task is a turn wasted; every lesson learned gets written here **the moment it is
learned** — by the orchestrator, on a worker's report (this file is locked to
workers).

Facts marked **[inherited]** come from previous-project rehearsals with the same
tools and must be re-verified by the Phase 01 environment spike; facts marked
**[verified]** have been confirmed in *this* repo, with the date.

## Codex CLI (`codex exec`) sandbox facts — codex-cli 0.137.0

- **[verified 2026-06-11, spike 01A]** `.git` writes are **ALLOWED** under
  `--sandbox workspace-write` (empty commit succeeded) — the inherited "blocked"
  fact is REFUTED for this CLI version. Codex workers use AGENTS.md's
  direct-commit path under `Codex Agent <agent@codex.local>`; COMMIT_PLAN.md
  remains the fallback if a future version re-blocks.
- **[verified 2026-06-11, spike 01A]** Non-destructive `&&` chaining WORKS —
  inherited "chained commands blocked" REFUTED. `rm -rf` is rejected by policy
  before execution (CONFIRMED) — use Node `fs` removals.
- **[verified 2026-06-11, spike 01A]** No browser available in-sandbox: no
  chromium binary; `npx --no-install playwright` fails. CONFIRMED — browser
  verification routes through the orchestrator/host.
- **[verified 2026-06-11, spike 01A]** Outbound HTTPS works with
  `-c sandbox_workspace_write.network_access=true`.
- **[verified 2026-06-11]** Host npm cache contains root-owned files →
  `npx` fails with EPERM in sandbox. Machine fix (human, optional):
  `sudo chown -R 501:20 ~/.npm`.
- **[verified 2026-06-11]** Token usage: the `turn.completed` JSONL event carries
  `usage:{input_tokens, cached_input_tokens, output_tokens,
  reasoning_output_tokens}` — ledger total = input + output.
- **[verified 2026-06-11]** `codex exec` reads stdin when invoked without
  redirection — **always append `< /dev/null`** in scripted invocations; omitting
  it caused a 15-minute no-event stall (observed).
- **[verified 2026-06-11, phase 02]** Nested `codex exec` (Codex launched from
  inside a Codex sandbox) fails when `~/.codex` is read-only ("failed to
  initialize in-process app-server client"). `scripts/codex-run.sh` handles this:
  temp writable `CODEX_HOME` + `auth.json` copy, cleaned on exit. Note: auth.json
  is briefly copied to a temp dir — local-only, deleted on exit.
- **[verified 2026-06-11, phase 02]** `rm -f` against `/private/tmp` paths is
  policy-rejected in-sandbox; use Node/Python fs cleanup.
- **[inherited]** Shared ambient auth does not reliably tolerate concurrent
  `codex` sessions — **one Codex session at a time** (not re-tested; keep until
  a deliberate probe says otherwise).
- **[verified 2026-06-11]** Stall rule works as written: no-event stall →
  relaunch identical brief once (with root cause fixed) → completed in ~7m.
- **[observed 2026-06-11, n=2]** Codex no-event stalls (2-event JSONL, then
  silence) occur intermittently even with stdin redirected; both recoveries
  succeeded on relaunch with `@brieffile` prompts. Prefer `@brieffile` over
  long inline prompts for codex-run.sh dispatches; keep the early-stall watch
  (static <500 bytes for 5m → alert) on every Codex dispatch.

## Cursor Agent CLI facts

- **[verified 2026-06-11]** On this machine, `agent` resolves to the **grok CLI**
  (`~/.grok/bin/agent`), NOT Cursor. The Cursor CLI is **`cursor-agent`**
  (`~/.local/bin/cursor-agent`, version 2026.05.24). All Cursor invocations must
  use `cursor-agent`.
- **[verified 2026-06-11]** `cursor-agent` has no `--trust` or `--workspace`
  flags; use `--print` (full tool access incl. write/shell), `--model <model>`,
  `--mode plan|ask` for read-only, `--output-format text|json|stream-json`, and
  set the working directory by `cd`-ing into the target worktree before invoking.
- Canonical invocation: `cd <worktree> && cursor-agent --print --model
  composer-2.5 "<bounded prompt>"`; read-only audits: `--mode=ask` with explicit
  no-edit instructions.
- **[verified 2026-06-11, spike 01B]** Cursor workers CAN mutate `.git` (commit
  succeeded, reverted) — the AGENTS.md direct-commit path for Cursor is valid.
- **[verified 2026-06-11, spike 01B]** `&&` chaining and outbound HTTPS both work
  in Cursor sessions; model identity `composer-2.5` confirmed; cwd = invocation dir.
- **[inherited]** macOS keychain errors on `agent status`/`agent models` → rerun
  with elevated access; record the occurrence here.
- Cursor sessions may run concurrently (separate worktrees); Cursor is the fan-out
  tool, Codex is the depth tool.

## Worktrees & repo facts

- **[observed 2026-06-11]** Parallel implement-workers on the SAME worktree:
  disjoint file ownership prevents edit conflicts, but `pnpm run check` runs
  collide with the sibling's half-written files. Either dispatch parallel
  implement tasks in separate worktrees, or instruct workers to gate only
  their owned test path (`pnpm test -- <owned dir>` + typecheck) and let the
  combined verification run the full gate after all siblings land.
- **[amended 2026-06-12]** Scoped gates MUST include lint on the owned path
  (`pnpm exec eslint <owned dir>`) — a scoped gate of typecheck+tests only let
  6 lint errors through to the sibling's full gate (15A).
- **[verified 2026-06-12]** `pnpm test -- <path>` does NOT filter — it runs the
  full vitest suite. The correct scoped form is `pnpm exec vitest run <path>`.
  Use that in scoped-gate briefs from now on.

- **[inherited]** `.env*` files are gitignored and do **not** propagate into new
  worktrees — the orchestrator copies them explicitly when (and only when) the
  brief requires a key.
- Worktree path convention: `../gg-wt/<phase>-<slug>`; branch `phase-XX/<slug>`.
- **[inherited]** Dev-server port collisions are common: read `${PORT:-3000}`,
  document an override; never hardcode a port.
- **[inherited]** A running/previously-run Next.js app pollutes typecheck via
  `.next/types/*` — clean `.next` before the typecheck gate.
- macOS shell: `date +%s` for timing (no `%N`); BSD userland (`sed -i ''` etc.).
- **[verified 2026-06-12]** pnpm blocks native build scripts by default and
  `pnpm approve-builds` is interactive (sandbox-hostile). Native deps
  (better-sqlite3) must be listed in `pnpm.onlyBuiltDependencies` in
  package.json.

## Gate commands (cheapest first — fail fast)

```
pnpm run typecheck   # tsc --noEmit (cheapest)
pnpm run lint        # eslint .
pnpm test            # vitest run (@live excluded unless CODEX_LIVE=1)
pnpm run check       # all three, in that order
```

**[verified 2026-06-11, phase 03]** — pnpm v10.28.2, TS 6.0.3 strict, vitest
4.1.8, eslint 10.4.1, zod 4.4.3. Dispatch workers via `scripts/codex-run.sh
<label> @<brieffile>` / `scripts/cursor-run.sh <label> <dir> "<prompt>"`.

## Update protocol

1. Worker hits an undocumented environment fact → states it in the final report
   under "environment discoveries."
2. Orchestrator adds it here (with date + [verified]) **before dispatching the
   next brief**. Encoding the lesson immediately is the habit that fixes most
   slowdowns — tribal knowledge is a defect.
3. The Phase 01 environment spike walks every [inherited] fact and flips it to
   [verified] / removes it.

# PROGRESS.md — Live Coordination

The single live coordination file for this repository. The orchestrator (CLAUDE.md)
owns it; workers update only the rows and log entries their brief tells them to.
This file records *state*, never *design* — design lives in the doc spine
(NORTH_STAR, TECH_SPEC, UX, WORLD, GAME_DESIGN) and `phase-plans/`.

## Phase Rotation Procedure

When a phase completes (all checklist items ticked with evidence, human accepted):

1. Archive this file's phase sections to `docs/progress-archive/PHASE-XX.md`.
2. Reset: Active Phase, Task Queue, Phase Checklist; keep Validation Log's last 5
   entries; carry Backlog forward untouched.
3. Update Active Phase to the next phase plan; copy its completion criteria into
   the checklist.
4. Orchestrator commits the rotation as a single commit: `Phase XX: close & rotate`.

# PHASE-30 — Floor Manifest Schema

## 1. Objective
The complete floor-manifest envelope — everything the Director may say, in one provider-accepted, gauntlet-validatable schema.

## 2. Context
GAME_DESIGN §12 (the what-and-how-much contract: layout knobs, theme, roster+placements, items, traps, 0–2 NPCs, quest, narration beats, metadata, signature flag); 29's findings (accepted constructs only); TECH_SPEC §6.

## 3. Dependencies
29.

## 4. Scope IN
- `src/schemas/manifest.ts`: the envelope composing PHASE-05 entity schemas; placement coordinates as floorgen-relative hints (room index + preference, not absolute tiles — engine resolves via 17's placement API); origin tags; callback references; protocol version stamp; signature-moment flag with its budget-relax marker.
- **Live contract test (`@live`, subtask 1): the full manifest schema accepted by the provider + one real generation parses.** Mock fixtures: 3 hand-written valid manifests (one per band) + a malformed set.

## 5. Scope OUT
- Prompt content (32). Gate logic (33/34). Application (35).

## 6. Owned files
`src/schemas/manifest.ts` (+ fixtures + tests).

## 7. Task breakdown
| Task | Type | Objective | Owned files | Agent | Est / Timebox | Parallel with |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | implement | **Live contract test first** — envelope skeleton accepted by provider | manifest.ts (@live test) | Codex | 10m / 20m | — |
| 2 | implement | Full envelope + fixtures + validation tests | manifest.ts + fixtures | Codex (same session) | 25m / 50m | — |
| 3 | verify | §12 coverage table (every manifest element ↔ schema field); re-run @live test; malformed set all rejected | — (read-only) | Cursor | 10m / 15m | — |

## 8. Verification commands
`pnpm run check` · `pnpm test -- --tag @live` (key present) · coverage table in report.

## 9. Completion criteria
1. Live test green: provider accepts the schema, output parses, Zod validates.
2. Every GAME_DESIGN §12 element expressible; nothing beyond it expressible.
3. Band fixtures validate; malformed set fails with usable error messages.
4. Acceptance bar: 31–35 build against a provider-proven shape; schema changes after this = protocol bump.

## 10. Risks & escalation
If a §12 element can't survive provider constraints (e.g., nesting depth), stop and report — the orchestrator + human decide the redesign; never silently drop an element.

item.text: I’m pulling the schema contract and materialization points next: the design section tells what must be expressible, and `floorgen/place.ts` plus the run provider show the names and shape downstream code already expects.

item.aggregated_output:
## 3. The Two-Layer World Model

The architecture has exactly one big idea, inherited from our reference projects and
sharpened: **separate creative intent from world physics.**

### Layer 1 — The Engine (deterministic, hand-written, trusted)

A finite, turn-based, seeded roguelike engine. It owns movement, combat resolution, line of
sight, item effects resolution, status effects, hunger/resources, win/loss, and rendering.
It is ordinary, well-tested software. It never calls an LLM. The game is fully playable
offline against a built-in fallback content pack — the AI is an enhancement, never a
runtime dependency for taking the next turn.

### Layer 2 — The Director (generative, AI, untrusted)

An LLM agent that authors content *between* floors, expressed only in a bounded content
language (the "floor manifest"): layout parameters, enemy definitions, item definitions,
NPC definitions with finite dialogue, quest definitions, event scripts, and narration. The
Director reads the player's trace and the run's memory, and writes the next floor.

Every manifest passes through a gauntlet before the player sees it (Section 5). A manifest
that fails any gate is repaired or replaced by fallback content — the player never sees a
broken floor, only a slightly less personal one.

### The freedom spectrum

"More freedom than Mystery Dungeon" is the point, so we are explicit about where freedom
lives:

| The Director MAY invent | The Director may NOT touch |
|---|---|
| New items: names, glyphs, effects composed from an effect vocabulary | The effect vocabulary itself (engine-owned verbs) |
| New enemies: stats, behavior parameters, abilities from a behavior vocabulary | Combat math, turn order, engine rules |
| New NPCs with finite dialogue and barter | Unbounded free-chat NPCs (post-MVP, behind the same gates) |
| Quests referencing real entities on real floors | Quests requiring entities that don't exist (integrity-checked) |
| Floor themes, layout parameters, events, narration | Map solvability (validated), player stats, inventory (engine-owned) |
| Story beats, callbacks, run-to-run memory hooks | Win/loss conditions, the existence of an ending |

The deliberate design bet: **composable vocabularies give the feeling of unbounded
invention with the safety of bounded execution.** A vocabulary of a few dozen effect and
behavior primitives, composed freely with AI-authored parameters, names, and fiction,
yields a combinatorial space no content team could exhaust — and every point in it is
something the engine already knows how to run.

---

## 4. Core Invariants (Never Break These)

1. The game is **finite** — explicit `WIN` / `LOSS` / `ABORTED` terminal states, fixed
   maximum depth per run.
2. The game is **turn-based** with **structured actions** — no real-time input, no
   free-text gameplay commands.
3. The engine is **deterministic and seeded** — identical seed + identical manifests +
   identical actions = identical run.
4. **AI output never mutates state directly.** It is parsed, validated, simulated, and
   applied by deterministic code, or it is discarded. The schema is the world's physics.
5. The game is **playable with zero API calls** — fallback content packs make the AI
   optional at runtime, mandatory only for the magic.
6. Every Director generation is **persisted as an inspectable artifact** — manifest in,
   gates passed, content out. Evidence over vibes.
7. **Playability is gated, not hoped for** — no floor reaches the player without passing
   the validation gauntlet.
8. The Director **cannot make the game unfinishable, unfair, or unfun by fiat** — bounds
   on stats, economy, and difficulty are enforced outside the model.

---

## 12. What the Director Actually Authors (the manifest, design view)

Per floor, the Director submits exactly: layout knobs (§2), theme/palette choice,
enemy roster + placements (within §9 budgets), item list + placements (§8), traps
(§10), 0–2 NPCs with dialogue/inventory/quest (§10), narration beats (floor intro
line + up to 3 [T] triggered observation lines), and metadata (origin tags,
callbacks referenced). Generated text caps [HARD]: narration lines ≤160
characters; entity/item/NPC names ≤40; descriptions and dialogue lines ≤200.
Schema detail is a phase-plan concern; *this* table of what-and-how-much is the
design contract the schema must express.

Per run, additionally: one **signature moment** (NORTH_STAR §6) — a single
flagged floor in the Middle band where budgets relax by 25% [T] and a named Made
entity is permitted. One per run [HARD].

---

# Phase 29 Ambient Director CLI Spike

## Question

Can `codex exec` serve as the Director's inference engine when invoked directly as:

```sh
codex exec --sandbox read-only -c approval_policy=never "<prompt>" < /dev/null
```

## Probe Setup

- Prompt: `runs/spikes/29-ambient-director/prompt.txt`
- Runner: `runs/spikes/29-ambient-director/run-probe.mjs`
- Validator: `runs/spikes/29-ambient-director/validate-manifest.test.ts`
- Validation schema: composed scratch `ManifestProbeSchema` importing actual `src/schemas` Zod exports for enemies, items, traps, NPCs, quests, narration, plus the run-loop `params` shape.
- Caveat: this repo currently has no exported `src/schemas/manifest` / `ManifestSchema`. The probe used the manifest-scale shape implied by `FloorContentProvider` plus `NarrationBeatsSchema`.

## Results

| Attempt | Latency (s) | Exit | Timed out | JSON parse | Zod validate | Failure reason |
|---|---:|---:|---|---|---|---|
| codex-1 | 0.227 | 1 | no | no | no | empty stdout; stderr: failed to initialize in-process app-server client |
| codex-2 | 0.230 | 1 | no | no | no | empty stdout; stderr: failed to initialize in-process app-server client |
| codex-3 | 0.226 | 1 | no | no | no | empty stdout; stderr: failed to initialize in-process app-server client |
| codex-4 | 0.227 | 1 | no | no | no | empty stdout; stderr: failed to initialize in-process app-server client |
| codex-5 | 0.224 | 1 | no | no | no | empty stdout; stderr: failed to initialize in-process app-server client |

Raw capture paths:

- `runs/spikes/29-ambient-director/attempts/codex-*/stdout.txt`
- `runs/spikes/29-ambient-director/attempts/codex-*/stderr.txt`
- `runs/spikes/29-ambient-director/attempts/codex-*/meta.json`
- Summary: `runs/spikes/29-ambient-director/run-summary.json`
- Validation summary: `runs/spikes/29-ambient-director/validation-results.json`

## Latency Stats

Codex attempts, wall-clock:

- n: 5
- min: 0.224s
- p50: 0.227s
- avg: 0.227s
- max: 0.230s

These are startup-failure latencies, not inference latencies.

## Parse / Validate Rates

- Codex JSON parse: 0/5
- Codex Zod validate: 0/5
- Cursor judge JSON parse: 0/1

## Failure Taxonomy

1. `codex exec` direct invocation failed before inference in all 5 attempts.
   - stderr included: `Reading additional input from stdin...`
   - terminal error: `failed to initialize in-process app-server client: Operation not permitted (os error 1)`
   - This matches the documented nested-Codex sandbox issue in `ENVIRONMENT.md`.
2. `cursor-agent --print --model composer-2.5` also failed before inference.
   - terminal error: `EPERM: operation not permitted, open '/Users/keigoshimada/.cursor/cli-config.json.tmp'`
   - This appears to be a Codex-worker sandbox permission issue for Cursor's config write.
3. No model output was produced, so there were no schema-shape failures to classify.

## Cursor Judge Probe

Command shape:

```sh
cursor-agent --print --model composer-2.5 "<100-word tone-verdict prompt>"
```

Result:

- latency: 2.257s
- exit: 1
- stdout: empty
- JSON parse: no
- failure: `EPERM: operation not permitted, open '/Users/keigoshimada/.cursor/cli-config.json.tmp'`

## Recommendation

NO-GO for the ambient adapter using direct `codex exec` from this worker sandbox. It achieved 0/5 parse and 0/5 validation, below the GO bar of at least 3/5 validate or 4/5 parse with fixable failure modes.

This result is a launch-path NO-GO, not a model-quality NO-GO. A follow-up host-side or wrapper-based probe could still test whether Codex can produce valid manifests once inference actually starts, but the direct command path specified here cannot serve as the Director engine in the current worker environment.

## Verification

```sh
node runs/spikes/29-ambient-director/run-probe.mjs
```

Result: completed; five Codex attempts and one Cursor attempt captured, all exit 1 with empty stdout.

```sh
pnpm exec vitest run --config runs/spikes/29-ambient-director/vitest.config.ts --reporter verbose
```

Result: 1 test file passed, 1 test passed. The validator wrote `validation-results.json`.

The initial direct Vitest invocation also confirmed root Vitest discovery excludes this owned scratch directory:

```sh
pnpm exec vitest run runs/spikes/29-ambient-director/validate-manifest.test.ts --reporter verbose
```

Result: no test files found because root config includes only `src/**/*.test.ts`; fixed by adding the local spike Vitest config.

## Scope Deviations / Risks

- Did not edit `PROGRESS.md` because the task owned files are limited to `runs/spikes/29-ambient-director/**`.
- Did not use `scripts/codex-run.sh` or alter `CODEX_HOME`; the brief specified the direct `codex exec` command.
- Did not update `ENVIRONMENT.md`; it is locked to workers. Environment discoveries are listed below for orchestrator ingestion.

## Environment Discoveries

- From a Codex worker sandbox, direct nested `codex exec --sandbox read-only -c approval_policy=never "<prompt>" < /dev/null` fails before inference with `failed to initialize in-process app-server client: Operation not permitted (os error 1)`.
- From the same sandbox, `cursor-agent --print --model composer-2.5` fails before inference trying to write `/Users/keigoshimada/.cursor/cli-config.json.tmp`.

## Actual Time

~12 minutes against a 15-minute hard timebox.

## Orchestrator Appendix — Host-Shell Re-Probe (the valid measurement)

The worker's 5 in-sandbox attempts measured the nested-Codex limitation, not the
question. Re-run from the host shell (where the game server would invoke it):

| Attempt | Latency | Exit | JSON parse | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| host-1 | 29s | 0 | OK | pure JSON, 9 keys, roster 4 / items 6 |
| host-2 | 26s | 0 | OK | roster 3 / items 6 |
| host-3 | 34s | 0 | OK | roster 4 / items 7 |

**VERDICT: GO.** 3/3 parse, ~30s median, $0 marginal. Full Zod validation pends
PHASE-30's manifest envelope (none exported yet — the worker's caveat stands).
Constraint: the ambient adapter must run host-side (or with the temp-CODEX_HOME
fix) — never from inside a Codex sandbox.

## 6. AI / Director Stack

- **Provider seam with two adapter classes** (swapping is config + eval run, not
  a refactor — NORTH_STAR §8):
  1. **Ambient-CLI adapter (default for dev/demo):** shells out to a locally
     authenticated coding CLI (`codex exec`, ambient ChatGPT login — effectively
     $0 marginal cost) with a JSON-demanding prompt; no provider-side schema
     enforcement — Gate 0 + the repair loop are the structured-output guarantee.
     Latency hides behind the prefetch window by design.
  2. **API adapter (Vercel AI SDK `generateObject`)** when keys are present;
     provider-side schema acceptance applies only to this path.
  Direct provider SDKs are allowed only inside the seam if the AI SDK blocks a
  needed capability.
- **Default models**: a frontier model for the Director (quality-critical, low call
  volume — one call per floor), a cheap/fast model for LLM-judge gates and bot
  players. Exact ids live in config, never in code, and are chosen by eval results.
- **Schemas: Zod is the single source of truth**, converted to JSON Schema for
  provider calls. Known landmines from the Guildmaster build apply: no root-level
  unions, every declared property required (nullable placeholders over optionals),
  `additionalProperties: false` throughout. Every schema gets a **live provider

item.text: The run loop’s current content interface places plain definitions today, but Phase 30 needs hints in the manifest. I’m keeping hints in the envelope alongside definitions so later materialization can pass them into `PlacementRequest` without changing entity schemas.

item.aggregated_output:
You are the Director for Everdeep, a finite seeded turn-based Mystery Dungeon roguelike. You author exactly one floor manifest between floors. The deterministic engine owns physics, combat, placement, RNG, win/loss, and validation. You never mutate game state directly. Your output is untrusted content data that must parse as JSON and validate against strict schemas. Use only structured manifest fields, closed vocabulary ids, bounded numbers, and finite dialogue. Do not explain anything.

The manifest shape is a JSON object with these required root fields: depth, band, params, roster, items, traps, npcs, quest, narration. params has bandOrSize, roomCountRange, flavor, and seed. For depth 3 use band and params.bandOrSize "shallows", roomCountRange {"min":4,"max":7}, flavor "open" or "warren", and a non-empty seed string. roster is an array of enemy definitions. items is an array of 4 to 8 item definitions. traps and npcs may be empty arrays. quest may be null. narration has floorIntro and observations.

Every tagged vocabulary object uses the provider-facing nullable-payload style: include the kind field, include every possible payload field, put exactly the active payload object on the matching field, and put null on every inactive payload field. Examples: a behavior with kind "approach_melee" must include approachMelee:{} and keepRange/fleeLowHp/packHunter/ambusher/territorial/guard/patrol/thief/caster/bodyguard/mimic:null. An effect with kind "nutrition" must include nutrition:{...} and all other effect payload fields null. A trigger with kind "quaff" must include quaff:{} and every other trigger payload field null. A targeting shape with kind "self" must include self:{} and every other targeting payload field null.

Use these closed ids and bounds for this request. Depth bands: "shallows", "middle", "lowest". Origins for generated enemies: "made". NPC origin, if any: "kept". Item kinds: "weapon", "armor", "charm", "draught", "note", "throwable", "food", "tool", "key_item", "coin". Behavior kinds: "approach_melee", "keep_range", "flee_low_hp", "pack_hunter", "ambusher", "territorial", "guard", "patrol", "thief", "caster", "bodyguard", "mimic". Effect kinds: "damage", "heal", "apply_status", "cure_status", "buff_stat", "nutrition", "teleport_self", "teleport_target", "blink", "knockback", "reveal", "identify", "enchant", "summon", "transform", "dig". Trigger kinds: "quaff", "read", "throw_hit", "equip_passive", "on_hit", "on_struck", "step", "use". Targeting kinds: "self", "melee", "bolt", "burst", "floor". Shallows enemy stats: hp 4-14, attack 2-5, defense 0-2, xpYield 2-6. Shallows item value coin: 5-30. Weapon attackBonus 1-6. Armor defenseBonus 1-5. Damage amount 1-8. Nutrition fullness 10-60. Dialogue and narration must be short.

Here is a compact valid example of the required field style:

item.text: The ambient host outputs are plain JSON and use the existing entity objects directly. To keep them close to valid, I’m planning `placementHint` as a required nullable extension on each manifest entry rather than wrapping every entity in a new object.

runs/briefs/phase30-manifest.md:6:OWNED FILES: src/schemas/manifest.ts (+ fixtures + tests), and an @ambient-tagged test file (env-gated like @live: runs only when AMBIENT_LIVE=1).
runs/briefs/phase30-manifest.md:13:5. @ambient test (AMBIENT_LIVE=1 only): one real codex exec call from the test (host-side; skip with a clear message if codex unavailable) → parseManifest → report validity; do not fail the suite on model variance — assert parse, record validity.
runs/briefs/phase31-seam.md:4:STEP 0: read runs/spikes/29-ambient-director/findings.md (orchestrator appendix: host-side constraint, ~30s latency) and src/schemas/manifest.ts (parseManifest is your output contract). ENVIRONMENT facts: codex exec needs `< /dev/null`; never invoke from inside a Codex sandbox (your own tests must MOCK the subprocess — spawn a fake codex via an injected exec function; one @ambient-tagged real-call test, env-gated AMBIENT_LIVE=1).
runs/briefs/phase31-seam.md:13:5. Tests (all mocked exec): taxonomy coverage (each failure path); timeout kills the process (fake hanging exec); parse pipeline; usage captured. @ambient: one real generateManifest (skip cleanly if codex absent), assert ok|taxonomy — never model-variance failures.
runs/briefs/phase03-scaffold.md:11:4. vitest.config.ts: include src/**/*.test.ts; `@live`-tagged tests excluded by default (env-gated: only run when CODEX_LIVE=1 or similar — document in the config comment).

Run Codex non-interactively

Usage: codex exec [OPTIONS] [PROMPT]
       codex exec [OPTIONS] <COMMAND> [ARGS]

Commands:
  resume  Resume a previous session by id or pick the most recent with --last
  review  Run a code review against the current repository
  help    Print this message or the help of the given subcommand(s)

Arguments:
  [PROMPT]
          Initial instructions for the agent. If not provided as an argument (or if `-` is used),
          instructions are read from stdin. If stdin is piped and a prompt is also provided, stdin
          is appended as a `<stdin>` block

Options:
  -c, --config <key=value>
          Override a configuration value that would otherwise be loaded from `~/.codex/config.toml`.
          Use a dotted path (`foo.bar.baz`) to override nested values. The `value` portion is parsed
          as TOML. If it fails to parse as TOML, the raw string is used as a literal.
          
          Examples: - `-c model="o3"` - `-c 'sandbox_permissions=["disk-full-read-access"]'` - `-c
          shell_environment_policy.inherit=all`

      --enable <FEATURE>
          Enable a feature (repeatable). Equivalent to `-c features.<name>=true`

      --disable <FEATURE>
          Disable a feature (repeatable). Equivalent to `-c features.<name>=false`

      --strict-config
          Error out when config.toml contains fields that are not recognized by this version of
          Codex

  -i, --image <FILE>...
          Optional image(s) to attach to the initial prompt

  -m, --model <MODEL>
          Model the agent should use

      --oss
          Use open-source provider

      --local-provider <OSS_PROVIDER>
          Specify which local provider to use (lmstudio or ollama). If not specified with --oss,
          will use config default or show selection

  -p, --profile <CONFIG_PROFILE_V2>
          Layer $CODEX_HOME/<name>.config.toml on top of the base user config

  -s, --sandbox <SANDBOX_MODE>
          Select the sandbox policy to use when executing model-generated shell commands
          
          [possible values: read-only, workspace-write, danger-full-access]

      --dangerously-bypass-approvals-and-sandbox
          Skip all confirmation prompts and execute commands without sandboxing. EXTREMELY
          DANGEROUS. Intended solely for running in environments that are externally sandboxed

      --dangerously-bypass-hook-trust
          Run enabled hooks without requiring persisted hook trust for this invocation. DANGEROUS.
          Intended only for automation that already vets hook sources

  -C, --cd <DIR>
          Tell the agent to use the specified directory as its working root

      --add-dir <DIR>
          Additional directories that should be writable alongside the primary workspace

      --skip-git-repo-check
          Allow running Codex outside a Git repository

      --ephemeral
          Run without persisting session files to disk

      --ignore-user-config
          Do not load `$CODEX_HOME/config.toml`; auth still uses `CODEX_HOME`

      --ignore-rules
          Do not load user or project execpolicy `.rules` files

      --output-schema <FILE>
          Path to a JSON Schema file describing the model's final response shape

      --color <COLOR>
          Specifies color settings for use in the output
          
          [default: auto]
          [possible values: always, never, auto]

      --json
          Print events to stdout as JSONL

  -o, --output-last-message <FILE>
          Specifies file where the last message from the agent should be written

  -h, --help
          Print help (see a summary with '-h')

  -V, --version

52604:Below are some default guidelines for determining whether the original author would appreciate the issue being flagged.
52612:5. The author of the original PR would likely fix the issue if they were made aware of it.
52613:6. The bug does not rely on unstated assumptions about the codebase or author's intent.

./runs/sessions/phase08-movement-r2.jsonl:20:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_9","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"sed -n '1,220p' NORTH_STAR.md\"","aggregated_output":"# Generative Gaming — North Star\n\n> **Can an AI agent author a living game world at runtime — items, enemies, quests, NPCs,\n> story — with enough freedom to genuinely surprise the player, while a deterministic\n> engine and an eval harness keep the game playable, fair, and finite?**\n\nThis document is the project's fixed point. It explains what we are building, why it is\ninteresting, and what must never break. It deliberately stays at low technical resolution:\nimplementation contracts live in `AGENTS.md` and `phase-plans/` (to be authored next).\n\n---\n\n## 1. The Concept\n\nWe are building a **Mystery Dungeon-style (不思議なダンジョン) roguelike where the dungeon\nis authored, floor by floor, by an AI Director while you play it.**\n\nIn a classic Mystery Dungeon game, procedural generation shuffles a fixed deck: the same\nenemies, items, and floor layouts in new arrangements. Here, the deck itself is generative.\nThe AI Director invents new items, enemies, quests, NPCs, floor themes, and story beats at\nruntime — but it can only express them through a strict, schema-validated content language\nthat a deterministic engine executes. The AI proposes; the engine disposes.\n\nThe one-line pitch: **a dungeon that reads you back.**\n\nThe Director watches *how* you actually play — your trace, not your words. Hoard\nconsumables and it sends enemies that punish hoarding and an NPC who mocks your full\npockets. Flee every fight and the dungeon grows narrow and pursuing. Talk to every NPC and\nthe story deepens around the characters you favored. Die, and your next run descends into a\ndungeon that remembers the last one. No two players experience the same dungeon, because\nthe dungeon is, in a real sense, *about them*.\n\n### Working fiction (revisable)\n\nWorking title: **Everdeep**. The dungeon is a vast, formless entity that shapes itself\naround each delver who enters — part labyrinth, part storyteller, part adversary. The\ndeeper you go, the more it knows you, and the more personal its inventions become. The\nfiction is intentionally thin scaffolding: it justifies \"the dungeon adapts to you\" inside\nthe story itself, so the AI's authorship is a feature of the world rather than a mechanic\nbolted onto it. Tone, naming, and story arc are decided later and are not load-bearing\nfor the architecture.\n\n---\n\n## 2. Why This Genre (Bottom-Up Reasoning)\n\nWe chose the genre from technical constraints first, exactly as instructed, and then made\nsure the constraints themselves produce the fun.\n\nA turn-based, grid-based, tile/text-rendered roguelike is the genre where today's LLMs and\nagent tooling are strongest and weakest in the right places:\n\n- **Turn-based** — no real-time latency pressure. The Director can think for seconds\n  between floors without the player ever waiting, because generation happens in the\n  background while the current floor is being played.\n- **Grid + structured actions** — game state is small, serializable, and inspectable.\n  Both humans and bot players consume the same structured action interface, which makes\n  automated evaluation (and agent-driven QA) cheap and honest.\n- **Seeded determinism in the engine** — the engine's randomness is reproducible. The only\n  nondeterministic component is the Director, and its output is captured as an artifact, so\n  every run can be replayed exactly.\n- **Floor boundaries** — natural generation checkpoints. Each floor is a self-contained\n  content unit that can be validated, simulated, and rejected *before* the player sets foot\n  on it. This is what makes runtime AI freedom safe.\n- **Tile/text-first rendering** — content is data, not assets. An AI-invented enemy needs a\n  glyph, stats, and behavior parameters — not a sprite sheet. This is what makes runtime AI\n  freedom *cheap*.\n\nThe genre's native expectations also align with generative content: roguelike players\nalready expect unfamiliar items with unknown effects, identify-by-use, permadeath, and\nruns that tell their own stories. AI-invented content feels like a deepening of the genre,\nnot a gimmick on top of it.\n\n### Where the \"genuinely interesting\" lives\n\n1. **Gameplay**: adaptive content keeps the difficulty curve and tactical texture personal.\n   The Director's job description is \"be a great dungeon master,\" not \"be hard\" — surprise,\n   tempo, and fairness over raw challenge.\n2. **Scenario**: emergent personal narrative. Quests, NPCs, and story beats are written in\n   response to your actual deeds, and the dungeon's memory persists across runs. The story\n   is not branching — it is *grown*.\n3. **Spectacle**: every floor manifest is an inspectable artifact. \"Show me what the AI\n   invented for me and why\" is itself part of the experience — a post-run \"dungeon diary\"\n   that recaps what the Director made of you.\n\n---\n\n## 3. The Two-Layer World Model\n\nThe architecture has exactly one big idea, inherited from our reference projects and\nsharpened: **separate creative intent from world physics.**\n\n### Layer 1 — The Engine (deterministic, hand-written, trusted)\n\nA finite, turn-based, seeded roguelike engine. It owns movement, combat resolution, line of\nsight, item effects resolution, status effects, hunger/resources, win/loss, and rendering.\nIt is ordinary, well-tested software. It never calls an LLM. The game is fully playable\noffline against a built-in fallback content pack — the AI is an enhancement, never a\nruntime dependency for taking the next turn.\n\n### Layer 2 — The Director (generative, AI, untrusted)\n\nAn LLM agent that authors content *between* floors, expressed only in a bounded content\nlanguage (the \"floor manifest\"): layout parameters, enemy definitions, item definitions,\nNPC definitions with finite dialogue, quest definitions, event scripts, and narration. The\nDirector reads the player's trace and the run's memory, and writes the next floor.\n\nEvery manifest passes through a gauntlet before the player sees it (Section 5). A manifest\nthat fails any gate is repaired or replaced by fallback content — the player never sees a\nbroken floor, only a slightly less personal one.\n\n### The freedom spectrum\n\n\"More freedom than Mystery Dungeon\" is the point, so we are explicit about where freedom\nlives:\n\n| The Director MAY invent | The Director may NOT touch |\n|---|---|\n| New items: names, glyphs, effects composed from an effect vocabulary | The effect vocabulary itself (engine-owned verbs) |\n| New enemies: stats, behavior parameters, abilities from a behavior vocabulary | Combat math, turn order, engine rules |\n| New NPCs with finite dialogue and barter | Unbounded free-chat NPCs (post-MVP, behind the same gates) |\n| Quests referencing real entities on real floors | Quests requiring entities that don't exist (integrity-checked) |\n| Floor themes, layout parameters, events, narration | Map solvability (validated), player stats, inventory (engine-owned) |\n| Story beats, callbacks, run-to-run memory hooks | Win/loss conditions, the existence of an ending |\n\nThe deliberate design bet: **composable vocabularies give the feeling of unbounded\ninvention with the safety of bounded execution.** A vocabulary of a few dozen effect and\nbehavior primitives, composed freely with AI-authored parameters, names, and fiction,\nyields a combinatorial space no content team could exhaust — and every point in it is\nsomething the engine already knows how to run.\n\n---\n\n## 4. Core Invariants (Never Break These)\n\n1. The game is **finite** — explicit `WIN` / `LOSS` / `ABORTED` terminal states, fixed\n   maximum depth per run.\n2. The game is **turn-based** with **structured actions** — no real-time input, no\n   free-text gameplay commands.\n3. The engine is **deterministic and seeded** — identical seed + identical manifests +\n   identical actions = identical run.\n4. **AI output never mutates state directly.** It is parsed, validated, simulated, and\n   applied by deterministic code, or it is discarded. The schema is the world's physics.\n5. The game is **playable with zero API calls** — fallback content packs make the AI\n   optional at runtime, mandatory only for the magic.\n6. Every Director generation is **persisted as an inspectable artifact** — manifest in,\n   gates passed, content out. Evidence over vibes.\n7. **Playability is gated, not hoped for** — no floor reaches the player without passing\n   the validation gauntlet.\n8. The Director **cannot make the game unfinishable, unfair, or unfun by fiat** — bounds\n   on stats, economy, and difficulty are enforced outside the model.\n\n---\n\n## 5. Harnessing the AI: The Gauntlet and the Evals\n\nThis is the project's center of gravity. Generative freedom is only worth having if the\ngame stays playable, so the harness is a first-class product, not test infrastructure.\n\n### Runtime gauntlet (every floor manifest, every time)\n\n- **Gate 0 — Structure.** Schema validation. Malformed output is rejected outright.\n- **Gate 1 — Legality.** Referential integrity (every quest target, NPC, and item the\n  manifest mentions actually exists in it or in the run) and hard bounds (stats, economy\n  values, counts, text lengths all inside engine-enforced ranges).\n- **Gate 2 — Playability.** The floor is simulated before it is served: a path to the\n  stairs exists, quest objectives are reachable, and a small ensemble of scripted bot\n  players completes the floor within a survivability band. Floors that are unwinnable or\n  trivially empty are rejected.\n- **Gate 3 — Quality.** Coherence and novelty checks: tone fits the run's fiction, content\n  is not a near-duplicate of recent floors, difficulty trend fits the run's curve. (Cheap\n  heuristics first; LLM-judge where heuristics can't reach.)\n- **Fallback.** A bounded repair loop, then graceful degradation to handcrafted content.\n  Failure is invisible to the player and loud in the logs.\n\n### Offline eval suite (every change to prompts, models, or schemas)\n\nA standing benchmark, run in CI and before any Director change ships:\n\n- A bank of recorded player traces and personas (hoarder, pacifist, speedrunner,\n  completionist, chaos gremlin) is fed to the Director.\n- Scored on: **validity rate** (gates 0–1), **solvability rate** (gate 2), **difficulty\n  band accuracy** (bot survival within target band), **novelty** (distance from content\n  bank and from the trace's previous floors), **responsiveness** (does the floor measurably\n  reference the trace it was given?), **latency and cost** per floor.\n- Thresholds are regression gates: a prompt \"improvement\" that drops solvability does not\n  merge.\n- Periodic agent-played full runs (an LLM player completing whole games) as an end-to-end\n  smoke of fun, not just function.\n\nA model or prompt change is treated exactly like a code change: benchmarked, compared,\naccepted on evidence.\n\n---\n\n## 6. The Player Experience (Demo Through-Line)\n\nThe five-minute experience the whole project optimizes for:\n\n1. Start a run. Floor 1 is gentle and watchful — the dungeon is learning you.\n2. Play a few floors. Notice the content bending: the items lean toward your style, an\n   enemy appears that counters your habit, narration nods at something you actually did.\n3. Meet an NPC who references your deeds and offers a quest that only makes sense for\n   *this* run.\n4. Hit the mid-run signature moment: the Director makes one bold, personal invention —\n   a named rival, a cursed gift, a floor built around your weakness.\n5. Win or die. Read the dungeon diary: what was invented for you, and what the dungeon\n   inferred about you to invent it.\n6. Start a second run. The dungeon remembers the first. The opening narration proves it.\n7. (For technical audiences) Open the artifact viewer: manifests, gates, rejected\n   generations, fallbacks — the machinery behind the magic, fully inspectable.\n\n---\n\n## 7. How We Build It: Agents Building the Agent Game\n\nThe project is also an exercise in agentic development. Two agent populations, two\ntimescales:\n\n- **Runtime agent** — the Director, inside the game, generating content for players.\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}
./runs/sessions/phase08-movement-r2.jsonl:29:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_14","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"sed -n '1,220p' UX.md\"","aggregated_output":"# UX.md — How the Game Feels to Play\n\nThis document describes the player's experience of the *machine*: screens, input,\nrhythm, feedback, and information. It deliberately ignores fiction, tone, and naming\n(NORTH_STAR §1 owns those). If NORTH_STAR is what we're making and TECH_SPEC is what\nit's made of, this is what it's like to sit in front of it.\n\nDesign stance in one line: **a fast, keyboard-driven, text-dense roguelike that feels\ninstant to play — with the AI's work surfaced as a quiet second layer you can always\npull open, and never forced to wait on.**\n\n---\n\n## 1. The Screen\n\nOne screen, no scrolling, desktop browser, dark by default. Three fixed regions:\n\n```\n┌────────────────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────┐\n│                                            │  HUD                 │\n│                                            │  depth · turn · HP   │\n│                THE GRID                    │  status · resources  │\n│         (tile glyphs, the world)           ├──────────────────────┤\n│                                            │  CONTEXT PANEL       │\n│                                            │  inspect · inventory │\n│                                            │  quest · dialogue    │\n├────────────────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────┤\n│  MESSAGE LOG (last ~6 lines, full history one keypress away)      │\n└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘\n```\n\n- **The grid** is the protagonist of the screen. Generous cell size, one glyph per\n  entity, color carries meaning (hostile / friendly / item / terrain / you). Unseen\n  tiles are dark, remembered tiles are dimmed, visible tiles are lit — the classic\n  three-state fog, readable at a glance.\n- **The HUD** is glanceable, not studied: depth, turn count, HP as number + bar,\n  active statuses as small labeled chips, key resources. Anything that changed this\n  turn pulses once.\n- **The context panel** is one space with modes (inspect, inventory, quest log,\n  dialogue). Only one mode visible at a time; the panel never stacks windows.\n- **The message log** narrates every turn in one terse line per event. It is the\n  game's voice; if something happened, it is in the log, always.\n\nNo modals over the grid during normal play. The player's eyes never need to leave\nthe grid to know they're safe; they look right when they want detail.\n\n## 2. The Input Model\n\nKeyboard-first, single-keystroke verbs, zero chording. The mouse works (click to\nmove/inspect) but everything is reachable without it.\n\n- **Arrows / WASD / vi-keys** — move (moving into an enemy attacks; into an NPC,\n  talks; into an item, steps on it).\n- **g** — pick up · **i** — inventory · **q** — quest log · **x** — inspect mode\n  (cursor roams the grid; every entity has a card) · **.** — wait · **>** — descend.\n- **Enter / Esc** — confirm / cancel, universally, no exceptions.\n- **Tab** — toggle the diary/artifact layer (§7). **?** — the full keymap, one page.\n\nRules the engine UI must obey:\n\n1. **Every keypress responds within one frame.** A turn resolves synchronously in the\n   client-side engine; there is no spinner anywhere in moment-to-moment play, ever.\n2. **Illegal inputs are explained, not eaten.** Bumping a wall says so in the log.\n   A disabled action shows *why* (\"too heavy to throw\") in its menu entry.\n3. **Dangerous turns ask once, inline.** Stepping into visible lava or attacking a\n   friendly prompts a one-line confirm in the log area (\"Really? y/n\") — not a modal.\n4. **Menus are lists, not trees.** Inventory and dialogue are flat, number-or-arrow\n   selectable, one level deep. Anything deeper is a design failure.\n\nAll input produces **structured actions** under the hood — the same actions bots use.\nIf a thing can be done, it appears in a menu or has a key; there is no hidden verb\nand no free-text input during play.\n\n## 3. The Turn Rhythm\n\nThe core loop runs at the speed of thought:\n\n> glance at grid → press key → world steps once → log line appears → glance → press.\n\nA practiced player takes 5–10 turns in ten seconds; the UI must never be the reason\nthat rhythm breaks. Animation is therefore *subordinate to* tempo: a melee hit is a\none-frame flash and a floating number; movement is instant repositioning with a\n~50ms ease; effects never queue input. Holding a direction key auto-repeats moves\nand auto-stops on anything notable (enemy sighted, item underfoot, HP threshold) —\nthe roguelike \"travel\" convention, conservative by default.\n\nWhen several things happen in one turn (you move, two enemies act, a status ticks),\nthe log renders them as discrete ordered lines in deterministic engine order, and\nthe grid shows the end state. The player can always reconstruct *what just happened*\nfrom the log alone.\n\n## 4. Knowing Things: Inspection as a First-Class Verb\n\nGenerated content means the player constantly meets entities they have never seen.\nThe UX answer is total inspectability:\n\n- **x + cursor** over anything shows its card in the context panel: glyph, name,\n  one-line descriptor, known stats, known abilities, and — crucially — *what the\n  player has learned vs. not* (\"unidentified: effect unknown\").\n- Items follow identify-by-use: an unknown item's card says exactly what is unknown.\n  Once used anywhere in this run, knowledge persists for the run.\n- Enemy cards accumulate observed facts (\"hits for 4–6\", \"fled at low HP\") rather\n  than revealing the stat block — the engine tracks what the player has witnessed.\n- Hovering (mouse) anywhere shows the same card. No information requires the wiki\n  that doesn't exist.\n\nThe contract: **the player may be surprised by content, never by rules.** Anything\nthe engine knows and fairness requires, the player can look up in two keypresses.\n\n## 5. Talking and Questing (the Structured Kind)\n\nDialogue is a context-panel mode, not a cutscene. Walking into an NPC opens their\ncard: portrait glyph, name, their lines rendered as short paragraphs, and a flat\nlist of 2–5 numbered replies. Replies are choices, not text entry. Esc leaves\nmid-conversation; the world is paused while talking (it is still your turn).\n\nThe quest log (q) lists active and completed objectives as checklist lines, each\nwith a \"where/what\" hint and a marker on the grid when the target is on the current\nfloor. Quest state changes announce themselves in the message log and pulse the HUD\nchip — quests never advance silently.\n\n## 6. Floor Boundaries: Where the AI Hides\n\nThe only place the AI could make the player wait — and the UX exists to make sure\nit never does.\n\n- Generation for floor N+1 begins the moment the player arrives on floor N\n  (TECH_SPEC §4). Median play time per floor exceeds generation time by design.\n- Taking the stairs plays a short, interruptible transition beat (1–2s): floor\n  number, a one-line whisper of what's below. In the common case the next floor is\n  already validated and waiting, and the transition is pure theater.\n- If generation *hasn't* finished: the transition holds up to a hard cap (~8s) with\n  a subtle progress shimmer, then the gauntlet's fallback floor serves instead.\n  The player sees a normal floor either way. **There is no \"AI is generating\"\n  spinner, no error toast, no broken floor — degradation is invisible at the table\n  and loud only in the artifact log.**\n- Floor arrival is a small ritual: grid fades in from the entrance outward, the log\n  prints the floor's one-line introduction, quest chips update. Three seconds of\n  orientation, then the rhythm resumes.\n\n## 7. The Second Layer: Diary and Artifacts (Tab)\n\nPlay never requires this layer; curiosity is rewarded by it. Tab flips the whole\nscreen (game is paused) to a two-tab surface:\n\n- **Diary** — a human-readable, scrolling recap of the run so far: per floor, what\n  was notable, written for the player. After death/victory this becomes the full\n  post-run diary (§8).\n- **Artifacts** — the engineer's view, read-only: each floor's manifest, which gates\n  it passed, what was rejected or repaired, fallbacks used, model id, timings. A\n  tree of plain documents, searchable, with a copy button. This is the\n  \"machinery behind the magic\" view from NORTH_STAR §6 — present for every run,\n  demo-ready by default.\n\nTab again returns exactly where you were. The layer is one keypress away and zero\nkeypresses in the way.\n\n## 8. Dying, Winning, and Starting Again\n\nPermadeath is honest but never abrupt:\n\n- The killing turn plays at full readable speed — the log shows exactly what killed\n  you; no \"you died\" before you saw why.\n- The death/victory screen is the **diary**, led by a summary strip (depth reached,\n  turns, kills, discoveries) and followed by the floor-by-floor recap. One more\n  screen follows: a short \"what the dungeon learned\" note — explicit, readable\n  evidence that the next run starts informed (the cross-run memory made visible).\n- One key starts the next run. Time from death to moving on a new floor 1: under\n  ten seconds, no menus in the way. A run's diary and artifacts remain browsable\n  from the title screen's run index.\n\nThe title screen itself is minimal: continue / new run / run index / settings.\nSettings fit one screen: volume-less (no audio in MVP), keybinding view, glyph size,\ncolor theme, message-speed and auto-travel toggles.\n\n## 9. First Five Minutes (Onboarding Without a Tutorial)\n\nNo tutorial mode, no instructional overlays. Instead:\n\n- Floor 1 of any first run is mechanically gentle (engine guarantee, not AI\n  goodwill) — few enemies, safe geometry, one of everything to learn on.\n- The first time each verb becomes relevant, the log offers a one-line hint\n  (\"an item lies here — g to pick it up\"), each hint shown once per profile, all\n  hints killable in settings.\n- **?** shows the keymap; **x** answers everything else. A player who has played\n  any roguelike is fluent in sixty seconds; a player who hasn't is fluent on\n  floor 2.\n\n## 10. Feel, Accessibility, and Non-Negotiables\n\n- **Latency budget:** input-to-grid-update < 16ms; stairs-to-playable < 2s typical,\n  8s worst case (then fallback). These are tested numbers, not aspirations.\n- **Readability:** glyph size adjustable; color is never the *only* channel (shape\n  and label always accompany it); the log is plain selectable text; full play is\n  possible at 125% browser zoom.\n- **Trust:** the log never lies and never omits — every state change the player can\n  perceive has a log line; replays (from the run index) re-render any past run\n  turn-by-turn from its trace, with the same UI.\n- **Quiet failure:** the player never sees a stack trace, a malformed floor, or an\n  apology toast.
